0: Life Cycle and Economic Assessment of Anaerobic Co-digestion of Dairy Manure and Food Waste
Rui Chen, M. Melissa Rojas-Downing, Yuan Zhong, Christopher M. Saffron, and Wei Ligo
Biosystems and Agricultural Engineering, Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI
1: A comprehensive life cycle and financial analysis was con- ducted to evaluate the environmental and economic perfor- mance of an anaerobic co-digestion system for dairy manure and food wastes versus a control system that anaerobically digests dairy manure but landfills food wastes. The environ- mental impacts of co-digestion are lower than the control system for global warming (66.95%), water eutrophication (18.88%), and smog emission (68.93%), but higher for air acidification (39.38%). Co-digestion results in a net energy ratio that is 1.67 times higher than the control system. An economic analysis revealed that co-digestion is more profit- able, as the calculated 25-year net value of over $8.4 million is far superior to the -$7.5 million for the control system. Considering both environmental impacts and economic indi- cators, manure and food waste co-digestion is a preferable solution for waste management and bioenergy production.
2: W aste management is crucial for community sus- tainability and human well-being. Agricultural waste alone is responsible for nearly 50% of methane and 60% of nitrous oxide global an- thropogenic emissions. 1 The development of recycling and low ecological footprint waste treatment has contributed to both climate change remission and increased energy independence. Anaerobic digestion (AD) is one such ecofriendly waste man- agement approach; it uses microorganisms to break down or- ganic matter in the absence of oxygen and generate biogas as a renewable energy source. This traditional yet effective tech- nique has been adopted globally by farms, municipalities, and industries for the improvement of their environmental respon- sibility and economic profitability. However, the traditional single-substrate, sole-purpose AD treatment is limited by low biogas production and poor economic performance, which hinders implementation of AD technologies.
Thus, co-digestion of multiple substrates has been suggested as one of the few approaches to enhance AD performance. 3,4 In

fact, it has been demonstrated that co-digestion maintains a more balanced nutritional composition and robust buffering capacity, which would support microbial activity for more effective di- gestion. 5,6 While most studies have been heavily focused on the technical aspects of co-digestion, a few have used life cycle as- sessment (LCA) for quantitative evaluation of the environmental impacts of anaerobic co-digestion. 3,4,7,8 For example, Lijo et al. analyzed the co-digestion of pig slurry and energy crops and demonstrated that co-digestion not only enhances energy pro- duction, but also reduces the disposal of solid wastes and generates a value-added fertilizer.9 Borjesson and Berglund carried out a fuel cycle emission analysis and determined that the environ- mental impacts are functions of the co-feedstock, the efficiency of biogas production, and development of end-use technologies. 10 De Vries et al. compared six different scenarios of manure di- gestion and co-digestion, and concluded that co-digestion of manure and other organic residues enhanced bioenergy production and environmental benefits. These studies went a step further, exploring energy and environmental aspects of the anaerobic co- digestion system; however, integrated analyses on the technical, environmental, and economic aspects of anaerobic co-digestion technology have not been comprehensively studied to date.

Besides AD technology, landfilling is another old but popular practice of waste treatment. Similar to AD, organic matter, deeply buried in a landfill site, can be converted into biogas. However, landfill gas recovery is considerably expensive and often absent in relatively small, less populated regions. For in- stance, among 2,435 active and more than 10,000 municipal landfill sites in the United States, only 638 sites are equipped with a biogas recovery system. 11 In 2012, released biogas from landfill sites was responsible for approximately 18.2% of the entire human-related methane emissions in the US. 12 Landfill leachate, a byproduct of organic waste decomposition, can leak into soil and groundwater, causing contamination. In compari- son, although AD might require higher capital investment, the physical boundary that separates the system from the environ- ment allows for better process control, resulting in a design that significantly minimizes contamination potential and maximizes biogas production. Previous work by DiStefano and Belenky compared the annual greenhouse gas emissions from a landfill site and an AD system using LCA. 13 They showed that new policies with incentives are needed to stimulate the transition from the current landfill-based municipal waste treatment sys- tem to an AD system. However, many previous studies are lacking comparisons that address both environmental and fi- nancial impacts of small-to-medium size, sustainable waste management operations.

0: This study aims to compare the life cycle and economic performance of two different scenarios for waste treatment at a hypothetical dairy farm located in the midwestern US. It focuses on sustainable organic solid waste management strategies that could be applied to facilitate widespread adoption of farm-based anaerobic co-digestion technology. Co-digestion of manure and food waste is compared to a control system.
1: <header>Goal, Scope, and Functional Unit for Life Cycle Assessment </header>

This LCA study followed the standard methodology provided by ISO 14040 series, Environmental Management-Life Cycle Assessment. Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data were obtained from the literature and theoretical calculations.

The goal of this assessment was to evaluate the environmental impacts of a medium-scale anaerobic co-digestion system using dairy manure and bakery process waste to produce electricity and agricultural coproducts. The objective was to determine how this co-digestion system (Scenario 1) could impact the environment and to compare these impacts with those of a conventional reference method (Scenario 0), which treated dairy manure in an AD system and disposed bakery process waste in an uncovered landfill site. The entire system includes raw ma- terial (manure and food waste) acquisition, manufacturing (AD/ AD + landfill, biogas purification, power co-generation, and digestate separation), local usage of biogas (electricity) and coproducts (heat, fertilizer, animal bedding), and recycle/waste management (heat and water) (Fig. 1). The hypothetical oper- ation occurred at a farm with a herd of 2,200 dairy cows located in the lower peninsula of Michigan. The food waste was pro- duced from a small bakery 50 km away from the dairy farm and the landfill site, although only the transportation of bakery process waste was included in the scope of this study.

The functional unit (FU) of this assessment was the total dry weight of dairy manure and bakery process waste produced annually. Quantitatively, the FU is 7,153 dry metric tons (t) of dairy manure and 2,382 dry t of food waste per year.

4: Biogas is produced by two plug-flow anaerobic digesters, located on-site, each with a capacity of 3,300 The hydraulic retention time (HRT) of the digestion process was 20 days. The storage pond for raw manure was uncovered, while the liquid effluent storage tank was covered. Both storage facilities had cement walls and floors; therefore, the storage pond was also responsible for greenhouse gas emissions, though had no eu- trophication potential. The cogenerator was assumed to have a 25% electricity efficiency. 14 The distance from the bakery to the dairy farm 0km) was the same as the distance from the bakery to the landfill site, and the fuel consumption of each round trip was applied. The AD system was assumed to be able to operate for at least 25 years; therefore, the inventory and the impacts caused by constructing the system (e.g., steel, cement, etc.) were not measured. The molecular formula for dairy manure was determined to be C3.63H6.19O3.03N0.16S0.02P0.02 and bakery pro- cess waste was (C6H10O5)n. 15 To ensure the authenticity of the
analysis, only current technologies, markets, and data were used in this study. The analysis and results were based on yearly operation without accidents.

6: The selection of feedstock is an important consideration in this assessment. It has been reported that dairy manure is a relatively low-energy-content feedstock even though large amounts are available. 16 The addition of starch-rich and other energy-rich organic wastes could result in a significant increase of biogas yield from AD. According to the biogas potential of different organic wastes, bakery food waste was one of the most biogas-producing organic wastes, making it ideal as a co-feed- stock with dairy manure. 3,17-19

A herd of 2,200 lactating dairy COWS was capable of pro- ducing 54,604 wet, or 7,153 dry t of manure annually (Table 1), and 100% of the animal waste was used for 14,20-34 Ad- ditionally, the state of Michigan regulates a maximum of 25% (v/v) non-farm substitution that can be co-fed into the com- mercial digesters. Therefore, a total of 3,970 wet or 2,382 dry t (Table 1) of bakery process waste were collected, either as supplement to the digesters (Scenario 1) or directly disposed of at landfill (Scenario 0). 21 Total solids (TS) content of the feed- stock is an important parameter for biogas production, and the recommended average of the TS loading in a plug-flow-type anaerobic digester was 12%.3 Additionally, the electricity consumed for feedstock mixing and pumping, and the fuel consumed for transporting food waste, were considered as part of raw material handling (Table 1). 14,22-24

2: Table 2 contains the mass balance information for both sce- narios. The most noticeable difference on raw material and che- mical uses between Scenario 1 and Scenario 0 was the water usage. Scenario 1 required around 16,000 more water (for the digestion operation) than Scenario 0. In addition, Scenario 1 also consumed more activated carbon than Scenario 0, due to the different H2S amounts between the two scenarios. Scenario 1 produced 5.02t of H2S, while Scenario 0 produced 1.75t. Liquid digestate and bed- ding material from Scenario 1 were 2,350 and 1,628 t TS/year, respectively, which were slightly higher than Scenario 0, with corresponding numbers of 2,229 and 1,345 t TS/year.

The energy balance included the electricity, heat, and fuel that crossed the system boundary. Following the energy balance, Scenario 1 was found to produce more electricity and heat than Scenario 0 (Fig. 2). Both scenarios had similar electricity, heat, and fuel consumptions. Therefore, there was more net energy generated from Scenario 1 (14.34 Gwh-e/year) than Scenario 0 5 Gwh-e/ year). The difference was also confirmed by the net energy ratio (total energy produced/total energy required); Scenarios 1 and 0 had net energy ratios of 4.54 and 1.70, respectively.

4: Four impact categories were chosen for Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) of the two scenarios: global warming po- tential (GWP), water eutrophication potential (WEP), air acid- ification potential (AAP), and smog potential (SP). The classification of each category is defined by the US Environ- mental Protection Agency (EPA), and the analysis was con- ducted using EPA's TRACI2 characterization factors. 40 Both contribution analysis and sensitivity analysis are presented to interpret the variables that affect each impact category.
5: GWP is the amount of greenhouse gases that are released during the life cycle of the process. Data were collected for CO2, CH4, and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions data were normalized to of CO2 equivalent (CO2-e) based on the following con- versions: kg CO2 kg CO2-e; 1 kg CH4 kg CO2-e; and kg N20=310kg CO2-e.3 Given the fact that man-made greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions are a main cause of global climate change, this impact factor was selected to represent the emission-reduction potential from implementation of AD.

According to mass balance data, Scenario 0 has an overall GWP of 2.7x104 t CO2-e, which is 67% more than that of Scenario 1 (1.6x104 CO2-e). Figure 3 demonstrates that AD in Scenario 1 contributes 87% of GWP, and Scenario 0 has 96% of GWP from landfill of food waste and AD of animal manure. Though there has not been any specific research published on the GWP from co-digestion of dairy manure and bakery wastes, the results from this study are comparable to other studies. Based on the data given in this study, co-digestion of 7,147 t dairy ma- nure and 2,382t food wastes produces 15,992 t CO2-e per year, which is 0.140t CO2-e/t of organic waste/month. According to a model provided by Zhang et al., 1,100 t of organic wastes, con- sisting of 80% dairy manure and 20% food waste (by mass), can generate around 170t CO2-e per month, which is 0.155 t CO2-e/t of organic waste/month. 41 Another study of a manure-only AD system shows that the GWP of AD of dairy manure only is 337 t CO2-e/year/100 cows, which can be further converted to 0.112t CO2-e/t of organic waste/month.4 42 The GHG emission intensity

for our study is 9.6% less than the co- digestion model, while 20% greater than the manure-only digestion sys- tem; however, the latter system did not take the GWP from propor- tional food waste into consideration, which might increase its final emis- sion significantly. This is further evidence that anaerobic co-digestion more capably mitigates global cli- mate change than does manure di- gestion alone.

A sensitivity analysis of GWP re- veals that the co-digestion system (Scenario 1) is more robust than the reference system (Scenario 0) (Fig. S1; Supplementary Data are available online at www.liebertpub.com/ind). Among six parameters-cow number, transportation distance, AD leakage, food waste amount, solids reduc- tion, and electricity production- cow number and solids reduction were the most sensitive parameters on GWP for both scenarios.

Chemicals such as ni- trogen and phosphorous, as well as operationally defined chemical ox- ygen demand (COD), were con- sidered the most significant WEP nutrients in this study. Land appli- cation of liquid digestate (fertilizer) and landfilling of food waste were the main contributors to WEP.

1: AAP is the potential change of atmospheric acidity caused by raw material storage and AD. NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx), and H2S generated from AD are usually considered as the major contribu- tors to air acidification. Even though more than 95% of H2S in collected biogas was removed by activated carbon, usually 1-3% of total biogas leaks into the atmo- sphere during digestion. 48

Compounds that can cause air acidification are converted into SO2- equivalent (SO2-e). The AAP is calculated using the following conversions: 1 kWh electricity consumed : 0.067 g SO2-e, 1 kWh electricity generated = 0.042 g SO2-e, and 1 kg fuel consumed=0.54g SO2-e. 49,50 Therefore, 1 dry t manure during AD produces 0.17kg SO2-e AAP, and 1 dry t AD effluent in land application produces 0.073 kg SO2-e AAP. 24,51 Figure 5 demonstrates the process contributions to AAP. 2: Smog is a form of air pollution consisting of plumes of nox- ious fumes. This phenomenon is caused by chemical reactions between sunlight, nitrogen oxides, and volatile organic com- pounds. 53 NOx and CH4 are the main chemicals capable of smog formation by the proposed scenarios. Figure 6 shows the SP distribution among different life cycle stages. Starting with raw material acquisition and AD, and ending with land application, smog emissions from Scenario 1 were slightly higher than from Scenario 0, as more methane production results in greater gas leakage. However, landfilling food waste in Scenario 0 pro- duced 706 CH4/year, or 2.59 Ox-equivalent (NOx-e)/year in terms of smog emission. The overall smog potential for Scenario 0 was 5.88 t NOx-e, which was much higher than for Scenario 1 (3.47 NOx-e/year).

The number of cows was the most sensitive model input, affecting SP for both scenarios (Fig. S4). Several studies have compared organic waste-treatment approaches such as AD, composting, incineration, and landfilling, and found that land- filling releases significantly more smog into the atmosphere. 54,55 Hung and Solli compared the life cycle of municipal organic waste through AD and incineration, and while the GHG emis- sions from both treatments were similar, AD significantly re- duced the formation of smog and particulate matter. 56 Saer et al. found that a well-controlled composting process was the best way to manage smog emissions; however, a composting system cannot be economically sustainable as it does not produce bio- gas for energy recycling. 57

1: One of the most important factors that determines the welfare of an economy is the quantity and environmental quality of its energy consumption. 59 Renewable energy must be both envi- ronmentally favorable and economically competitive to draw investors' attention. 60 Despite the fact that alternative energy's positive influence on climate change alleviation is an undeniable truth, many projects currently require high capital and/or oper- ating expenditures. 61,62 In comparison, AD is a well-established and low-cost technology for converting organic waste into heat and electricity. This technology ensures both supply and de- mand of renewable products (energy, natural fertilizer, etc.), which could facilitate improved economic welfare. 63 Cost and profit analysis examines capital expenditures (capex), operating expenditures (opex), and revenue of a proposed system. Payback period was calculated based on the capital investment and total net profit. In addition, an economic sensitivity analysis was conducted to compare both scenarios.

cation and global warming are key impact factors for this study, and the co-digestion system is a bet- ter option to reduce environmental impacts from organic waste han- dling. In addition, the net energy export of the co-digestion system is 14.34 GWh electricity equivalent, which is significantly higher than 2.65 GWh electricity equivalent from the control system. The eco- nomic analysis also supports the use of co-digestion based on greater profitability. Revenue from both scenarios is mainly generated from electricity and fertilizer produc- tion. The co-digestion system has a payback period of approximately 6 years, while the control system

0: <title>ANAEROBIC CO-DIGESTION OF DAIRY MANURE AND FOOD WASTE </title>

cannot create positive net cash outflow without financial in- centives. The most sensitive variables affecting profitability are the electricity price and amount of electricity. These analyses clearly demonstrate that co-digestion of manure and food waste outcompetes the control system in terms of environmental im- pacts and economic indicators. As such, co-digestion should be considered by any medium-sized modern farm as an environ- mentally sustainable way of enhancing farm profits.
